If 100% of young men get treated and 0% don’t, there are no untreated young men to compare to – you are stuck. You need people in BOTH groups for every “type” of person.

Assumption 3: SUTVA

No interference, no hidden versions (covered above).

“Strong Ignorability” = Ignorability + Overlap Together

Some lectures use the term strong ignorability to mean assumptions 1 and 2 combined. If someone says “assume strong ignorability,” they mean both \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid X\) AND \(0 < \Pr(Z\!=\!1 \mid X) < 1\).

Notation reminder: \(A \perp\!\!\!\perp B \mid C\) means “if you already know \(C\), then learning \(B\) tells you nothing new about \(A\).” For example, if \(C\) = your background, \(B\) = whether you got treated, and \(A\) = your potential outcomes: once I know your background, finding out whether you actually got treated doesn’t change my prediction of your outcomes.

8.4 Identification: How These Assumptions Let You Estimate Causal Effects

Under these three assumptions:

\[\tau = \mathbb{E}\Big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1, X] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=0, X]\Big]\]

The logic (important for the exam – know this chain):

What we want: \(\mathbb{E}[Y(1)]\) – the average outcome if everyone were treated. We can never see this directly. But we CAN get it from observable data:

  1. \(\mathbb{E}[Y(1)] = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y(1) \mid X]\big]\) – start by breaking the overall average into subgroup averages (tower property: “average of the averages”)
  2. \(= \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y(1) \mid Z=1, X]\big]\) – ignorability lets us add “\(Z=1\)” without changing anything (within each \(X\) group, treatment is as-good-as-random, so conditioning on getting treated doesn’t bias things)
  3. \(= \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1, X]\big]\) – consistency: for treated people, \(Y = Y(1)\), so we swap the unobservable \(Y(1)\) for the actual observed outcome \(Y\)

Do the same thing for \(Y(0)\) (conditioning on \(Z=0\)). Subtract: \(\tau = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=1, X] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z=0, X]\big]\).

The key insight: We started with something we can never observe (\(Y(1)\) for everyone) and ended up with something we CAN compute from data (average outcome of treated people vs. controls, within each \(X\) group, averaged across all \(X\) groups). The assumptions bridge the gap between the imaginary and the real.

Getting ATT and ATC: Same idea, but you change who you average over:

\[\text{ATT} = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!1, X] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!0, X] \;\big|\; Z = 1\big]\] \[\text{ATC} = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!1, X] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!0, X] \;\big|\; Z = 0\big]\]

The inner comparison (treated vs. control outcomes for people with the same background) is the same. The difference is who you weight over at the end: ATT averages over the background distribution of the treated people, ATC over the control people, and ATE over everyone.

You can also do this with just the propensity score instead of all of \(X\):

\[\tau = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!1, e(X)] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!0, e(X)]\big]\]

This works because the balancing property says \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid e(X)\) – conditioning on the PS is enough, you don’t need all of \(X\).

8.5 The Propensity Score

What is it?

\[e(X) = \Pr(Z = 1 \mid X)\]

The probability of getting treated, given your background.

Why is it useful?

The Rosenbaum-Rubin Balancing Property:

\[(Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid e(X)\]

Instead of trying to match on 15 different covariates at once (age, income, education, race, …), you can collapse all of them into this single number and match on that. If two people have the same propensity score, their covariates are balanced on average – even though the two people might have different specific characteristics.

Think of it like a credit score: instead of a bank looking at your income, debt, payment history, etc. separately, they roll everything into one number. The propensity score does the same thing for treatment probability.

It is the coarsest balancing score – the most compressed summary of all your background info that still gets the job done. You could match on the full vector \(X\) (that trivially works), but that is impractical with many variables. The propensity score is the minimum you need.

Proof sketch of the balancing property (exam-ready)

We want to show: within a group of people who all have the same propensity score, covariates are balanced between treated and control. In math: \(X \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid e(X)\).

Here is the idea. Take everyone with \(e(X) = 0.3\) (30% chance of treatment). Within this group, every person has the same 30% chance of being treated, regardless of their specific \(X\) values. So who gets treated is like a coin flip with probability 0.3 – it has nothing to do with the specific covariates. Treated people within this group are basically a random subset.

Formally: fix \(e(X) = p\). Among all people with this PS value:

\[\Pr(X = x \mid Z = 1, e(X) = p) = \frac{\Pr(Z=1 \mid X=x) \Pr(X=x)}{\Pr(Z=1 \mid e(X)=p)} = \frac{p \cdot \Pr(X = x)}{p} = \Pr(X = x \mid e(X) = p)\]

The key: \(\Pr(Z=1 \mid X=x) = e(x) = p\) for everyone in this group (that is what it MEANS to have the same PS). So the \(p\) in the numerator and denominator cancels, and the distribution of \(X\) among the treated is the same as the distribution of \(X\) in the whole group.

Visualizing the Propensity Score

N_ps <- 600
age_ps  <- rnorm(N_ps, 45, 10)
male_ps <- rbinom(N_ps, 1, 0.5)
inc_ps  <- rnorm(N_ps, 50000, 15000)

lp_ps <- -3 + 0.04 * age_ps + 0.5 * male_ps + 0.00002 * inc_ps
Z_ps <- rbinom(N_ps, 1, plogis(lp_ps))

ps_model <- glm(Z_ps ~ age_ps + male_ps + inc_ps, family = binomial)
ps_vals <- fitted(ps_model)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5.5, 4, 3, 1))

# Panel 1: PS histograms by group
hist(ps_vals[Z_ps == 1], breaks = 25, col = adjustcolor("firebrick", 0.5),
     border = "white", probability = TRUE, xlim = c(0, 1),
     main = "Propensity Score by Group",
     xlab = expression(hat(e)(X)))
hist(ps_vals[Z_ps == 0], breaks = 25, col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.5),
     border = "white", probability = TRUE, add = TRUE)
legend("topright", legend = c("Treated", "Control"),
       fill = adjustcolor(c("firebrick", "steelblue"), 0.5), bty = "n")

# Panel 2: balance within PS quintiles
ps_quintile <- cut(ps_vals, breaks = quantile(ps_vals, probs = 0:5 / 5), include.lowest = TRUE)
smd_overall <- (mean(age_ps[Z_ps == 1]) - mean(age_ps[Z_ps == 0])) /
               sqrt((var(age_ps[Z_ps == 1]) + var(age_ps[Z_ps == 0])) / 2)
smd_within <- sapply(levels(ps_quintile), function(q) {
  idx <- ps_quintile == q
  if (sum(Z_ps[idx] == 1) > 1 & sum(Z_ps[idx] == 0) > 1) {
    a_t <- age_ps[idx & Z_ps == 1]; a_c <- age_ps[idx & Z_ps == 0]
    (mean(a_t) - mean(a_c)) / sqrt((var(a_t) + var(a_c)) / 2)
  } else NA
})

barplot(c(smd_overall, smd_within), col = c("coral", rep("steelblue", 5)),
        main = "Age SMD: Before vs Within PS Quintiles",
        ylab = "SMD (Age)", names.arg = c("Before", paste0("Q", 1:5)), las = 2)
abline(h = c(-0.1, 0.1), lty = 3, col = "firebrick")

What you see: Left: PS distributions overlap – good (overlap check)! Right: the overall age imbalance (coral) shrinks dramatically within each PS quintile (blue). The propensity score creates balance.

Estimating the propensity score

In practice, use logistic regression:

\[\Pr(Z = 1 \mid X) = \frac{\exp(\alpha + \beta^\top X)}{1 + \exp(\alpha + \beta^\top X)}\]

Important: The goal is NOT prediction accuracy. The goal is covariate balance after matching or weighting.

How to interpret coefficients: They tell you what predicts SELECTION into treatment, NOT causal effects. A large positive coefficient on age means older people are more likely to be treated.

Don’t include instruments (things that affect \(Z\) but not \(Y\)) – they increase variance without reducing bias.

Always check: overlap (PS histograms) and balance (SMDs after matching/weighting).

9. Week 7: Estimating Causal Effects – IPW, AIPW, and Diagnostics

9.1 Design vs. Analysis: A Two-Phase Approach

Design phase (NO outcomes): Estimate PS, match/weight, check balance (SMDs, Love plots, overlap). Iterate until balance is good.

Analysis phase (NOW use Y): Estimate \(\hat{\tau}\), build CI.

Why separate? If you look at outcomes first, you might choose the method that gives the answer you want. This mirrors how clinical trials lock in the analysis plan before unblinding.

Concrete example of what goes wrong: Suppose during matching you notice that some treated units have very low outcomes, making them “hard to match.” If you drop them (because their outcomes look weird), you bias the estimated effect upward – you’ve cherry-picked the treated units with good outcomes. This is exactly the kind of fishing that the design/analysis separation prevents.

Visualizing the Pipeline

par(mar = c(1, 1, 2, 1))
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 10), xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n",
     xlab = "", ylab = "", main = "The Observational Study Pipeline", bty = "n")

# Design phase
rect(0.5, 5.5, 4.5, 9.5, col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.15), border = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
text(2.5, 9.2, "DESIGN PHASE", font = 2, col = "steelblue", cex = 1.1)
text(2.5, 8.3, "Uses: X, Z only", cex = 0.8, col = "gray30")
text(2.5, 7.6, "1. Estimate PS", cex = 0.8)
text(2.5, 7.0, "2. Match / Weight", cex = 0.8)
text(2.5, 6.4, "3. Check Balance (SMD)", cex = 0.8)
text(2.5, 5.8, "4. Check Overlap", cex = 0.8)

# Arrow
arrows(2.5, 5.3, 2.5, 4.7, lwd = 3, col = "firebrick", length = 0.15)
text(4.2, 5.0, "SACRED\nBOUNDARY", col = "firebrick", font = 2, cex = 0.8)

# Analysis phase
rect(0.5, 0.5, 4.5, 4.5, col = adjustcolor("seagreen", 0.15), border = "seagreen", lwd = 2)
text(2.5, 4.2, "ANALYSIS PHASE", font = 2, col = "seagreen", cex = 1.1)
text(2.5, 3.3, "NOW uses: Y", cex = 0.8, col = "gray30")
text(2.5, 2.6, "5. Estimate ATE", cex = 0.8)
text(2.5, 2.0, "6. Build 95% CI", cex = 0.8)
text(2.5, 1.4, "7. Sensitivity Analysis", cex = 0.8)

# DO NOT box
rect(5.5, 5.5, 9.5, 9.5, col = adjustcolor("coral", 0.1), border = "coral", lwd = 2)
text(7.5, 9.2, "DO NOT", font = 2, col = "firebrick", cex = 1.1)
text(7.5, 8.3, "Look at Y during design", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 7.6, "Compute ATE during design", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 7.0, "Pick model based on result", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 6.4, "This = fishing / p-hacking", cex = 0.8)

# Analogy box
rect(5.5, 0.5, 9.5, 4.5, col = adjustcolor("mediumpurple", 0.1), border = "mediumpurple", lwd = 2)
text(7.5, 4.2, "ANALOGY", font = 2, col = "mediumpurple", cex = 1.1)
text(7.5, 3.3, "Clinical trial:", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 2.6, "Lock in the analysis plan", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 2.0, "BEFORE unblinding the data", cex = 0.8)
text(7.5, 1.4, "Same logic here.", cex = 0.8)

9.2 Subclassification (Stratification)

Instead of matching individual pairs, you can sort everyone into buckets based on their propensity score (e.g., 5 groups: the bottom 20%, next 20%, etc.). Within each bucket, treated and control people have similar PS values, so they are roughly comparable. Then you compute the treatment effect within each bucket and take a weighted average:

\[\hat{\tau}_{\text{strat}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{N_k}{N} \left(\bar{Y}_{T,k} - \bar{Y}_{C,k}\right)\]

A classic result (Cochran 1968): just 5 buckets removes about 90% of the bias. More buckets = even less bias, but each bucket has fewer people in it so the estimates within each bucket get noisier.

Watch out for sparse buckets: If one bucket has, say, 1 treated person and 42 controls, the treatment effect estimate for that bucket is based on a single person – basically useless. Fix: Merge it with a neighboring bucket, or just use fewer buckets overall.

If your PS model is wrong: Garbage in, garbage out. Stratifying on a badly estimated PS can actually make things WORSE than not stratifying at all. Always check balance within your strata.

9.3 Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW)

IPW reweights every person so that the weighted groups look like a randomized experiment.

The intuition – a concrete example

  • College-educated people: 80% enroll in training (\(e(X) = 0.8\))
  • Non-college people: 20% enroll (\(e(X) = 0.2\))

The treated group is 80% college. That does NOT look like the population. IPW fixes this:

  • Treated college person: weight \(1/0.8 = 1.25\) (over-represented, downweight)
  • Treated non-college person: weight \(1/0.2 = 5\) (rare, upweight a lot)

After reweighting, both groups look like the overall population.

The IPW estimator

\[\hat{\tau}_{\text{IPW}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\frac{Z_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-Z_i)Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\]

IPW estimator for the ATT:

\[\hat{\tau}_{\text{ATT}} = \frac{1}{n_1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[Z_i Y_i - \frac{\hat{e}(X_i)(1-Z_i)Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\]

The control group is reweighted so the reweighted controls look like the treated group. This is useful when the estimand of interest is the effect on those who actually received treatment.

Why IPW is unbiased (key proof)

The idea: when you divide by \(e(X)\), the propensity score in the numerator (from \(\mathbb{E}[Z \mid X] = e(X)\)) cancels with the one in the denominator, leaving you with exactly what you want.

\[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{Z_i Y_i}{e(X_i)}\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{e(X_i) \cdot \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) \mid X_i]}{e(X_i)}\right] = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1)]\]

Step by step: use the tower property (condition on \(X\) first). Given \(X\), \(Z\) is just a coin flip with probability \(e(X)\), and \(Y_i(1)\) is fixed. So \(\mathbb{E}[Z_i Y_i(1) \mid X_i] = e(X_i) \cdot \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1) \mid X_i]\). Divide by \(e(X_i)\) and the PS cancels perfectly.

IPW achieves covariate balance as a theorem

If the PS is correct, the IPW-weighted treated group has the same average of EVERY covariate as the overall population. Same for the control group. This is not a hope – it is a guaranteed mathematical fact.

Full proof (know this for the exam): For any function \(g(X)\) (could be \(X\) itself, \(X^2\), \(\log X\), whatever):

\[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{Z \cdot g(X)}{e(X)}\right] = \mathbb{E}\left[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{Z \cdot g(X)}{e(X)} \,\Big|\, X\right]\right] \quad\text{(tower property: break into subgroup averages)}\]

\[= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{g(X)}{e(X)} \cdot \mathbb{E}[Z \mid X]\right] \quad\text{(given X, g(X) and e(X) are just numbers, pull them out)}\]

\[= \mathbb{E}\left[\frac{g(X)}{e(X)} \cdot e(X)\right] = \mathbb{E}[g(X)] \quad\text{(e(X) cancels!)}\]

This works for ANY \(g\): \(g(X) = X\), \(g(X) = X^2\), \(g(X) = \sin(X)\), anything. So IPW-weighted treated group has the same mean of every function of covariates as the population. If this doesn’t hold in your data, your PS model is wrong.

Expected value of IPW weights: E[w_i] = 2 (exam proof)

Each person’s IPW weight has two pieces: one for being treated (\(Z/e(X)\)) and one for being control (\((1-Z)/(1-e(X))\)). Only one piece is ever “on” for a given person (either \(Z=1\) or \(Z=0\)). But on average, each piece contributes 1, so the average weight is 2.

Define the IPW weight for ATE as \(w_i = \frac{Z_i}{e(X_i)} + \frac{1-Z_i}{1-e(X_i)}\). Then:

\[\mathbb{E}[w_i] = \mathbb{E}\left[\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{Z_i}{e(X_i)} + \frac{1-Z_i}{1-e(X_i)} \;\Big|\; X_i\right]\right] \quad\text{(tower property)}\]

Given \(X_i\): \(\mathbb{E}[Z_i/e(X_i) \mid X_i] = e(X_i)/e(X_i) = 1\), and \(\mathbb{E}[(1-Z_i)/(1-e(X_i)) \mid X_i] = (1-e(X_i))/(1-e(X_i)) = 1\).

So \(\mathbb{E}[w_i] = \mathbb{E}[1 + 1] = 2\).

Effective Sample Size (ESS) – why weight variability hurts

Think of it this way: if one person has weight 100 and everyone else has weight 2, that one person basically IS your entire estimate. You technically have \(N\) people in your sample, but it is as if you only have a few because one person’s outcome is drowning out everyone else’s.

The effective sample size tells you how many equally-weighted people your weighted sample is actually worth:

\[\text{ESS} \approx \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^2}\]

If all weights are equal (\(w_i = c\)), then \(\text{ESS} = (Nc)^2/(Nc^2) = N\) – full sample. If a few weights are very large, \(\sum w_i^2\) is dominated by the big ones and ESS shrinks dramatically.

Concrete example (from exam review): Suppose \(N\) units, half with \(e(X) = 0.5\) and half with \(e(X) = 0.01\).

  • Units with \(e(X) = 0.5\): \(w_i = Z_i/0.5 + (1-Z_i)/0.5 = 2\) regardless of treatment.
  • Units with \(e(X) = 0.01\), treated (\(Z_i = 1\)): \(w_i = 1/0.01 = 100\).
  • Units with \(e(X) = 0.01\), control (\(Z_i = 0\)): \(w_i = 1/0.99 \approx 1.01\).

The treated units with \(e(X) = 0.01\) have weight 100 – they each “count” as much as 50 normal-weight units. These few extreme weights dominate \(\sum w_i^2\), making ESS far smaller than \(N\). The estimator is technically unbiased but practically useless due to enormous variance.

Visualizing IPW: How Reweighting Creates Balance

N_ipw <- 500
X_ipw <- rnorm(N_ipw, 0, 1)
ps_true_ipw <- plogis(1.5 * X_ipw)
Z_ipw <- rbinom(N_ipw, 1, ps_true_ipw)
w_ipw <- ifelse(Z_ipw == 1, 1 / ps_true_ipw, 1 / (1 - ps_true_ipw))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 4, 3, 1))

# Panel 1: PS vs weight
plot(ps_true_ipw, w_ipw, pch = 16,
     col = adjustcolor(ifelse(Z_ipw == 1, "firebrick", "steelblue"), 0.4),
     cex = 0.7, log = "y",
     main = "IPW Weight vs Propensity Score",
     xlab = expression(e(X)), ylab = "Weight (log scale)")
legend("top", legend = c("Treated", "Control"),
       col = c("firebrick", "steelblue"), pch = 16, bty = "n")
mtext("Near 0 or 1 = extreme weights!", side = 1, line = 3.8, cex = 0.7, col = "gray30")

# Panel 2: balance before and after weighting
mean_x_t_raw <- mean(X_ipw[Z_ipw == 1])
mean_x_c_raw <- mean(X_ipw[Z_ipw == 0])
mean_x_t_ipw <- sum(Z_ipw * X_ipw / ps_true_ipw) / sum(Z_ipw / ps_true_ipw)
mean_x_c_ipw <- sum((1 - Z_ipw) * X_ipw / (1 - ps_true_ipw)) / sum((1 - Z_ipw) / (1 - ps_true_ipw))

vals_ipw <- matrix(c(mean_x_t_raw, mean_x_c_raw, mean_x_t_ipw, mean_x_c_ipw),
               nrow = 2, dimnames = list(c("Treated", "Control"), c("Unweighted", "IPW-Weighted")))
barplot(vals_ipw, beside = TRUE, col = c("firebrick", "steelblue"),
        main = "Covariate Mean of X by Group", ylab = "Mean of X")
abline(h = mean(X_ipw), lty = 2, col = "seagreen", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("Treated", "Control", "Population mean"),
       fill = c("firebrick", "steelblue", NA), lty = c(NA, NA, 2),
       col = c(NA, NA, "seagreen"), lwd = c(NA, NA, 2),
       border = c("black", "black", NA), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

What you see: Left: when PS is near 0 or 1, weights explode (note log scale). Right: before weighting, treated has higher X. After IPW, both match the population mean (green dashed).

9.4 The Problem with Extreme Weights

If \(e(X) \approx 0\) or \(1\), weights blow up. One person can dominate the entire estimate.

Visualizing Extreme Weights and the Overlap Problem

N_ew <- 400
X_ew <- c(rnorm(200, -1, 0.8), rnorm(200, 1, 0.8))
ps_ew <- plogis(2 * X_ew)
Z_ew <- rbinom(N_ew, 1, ps_ew)
Y0_ew <- 2 * X_ew + rnorm(N_ew, 0, 2)
Y1_ew <- Y0_ew + 3
Y_ew <- ifelse(Z_ew == 1, Y1_ew, Y0_ew)

ipw_raw <- mean(Z_ew * Y_ew / ps_ew - (1 - Z_ew) * Y_ew / (1 - ps_ew))

keep_ew <- ps_ew > 0.1 & ps_ew < 0.9
ipw_trimmed <- mean(Z_ew[keep_ew] * Y_ew[keep_ew] / ps_ew[keep_ew] -
                    (1 - Z_ew[keep_ew]) * Y_ew[keep_ew] / (1 - ps_ew[keep_ew]))

par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(5, 4, 3, 1))

# Panel 1: PS histogram showing poor overlap
hist(ps_ew[Z_ew == 1], breaks = 30, col = adjustcolor("firebrick", 0.5),
     border = "white", probability = TRUE, xlim = c(0, 1),
     main = "Poor Overlap: PS Distributions",
     xlab = expression(hat(e)(X)), ylim = c(0, 5))
hist(ps_ew[Z_ew == 0], breaks = 30, col = adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.5),
     border = "white", probability = TRUE, add = TRUE)
rect(0, 0, 0.1, 5, col = adjustcolor("gray", 0.3), border = NA)
rect(0.9, 0, 1, 5, col = adjustcolor("gray", 0.3), border = NA)
legend("top", legend = c("Treated", "Control", "Trim zone"),
       fill = c(adjustcolor("firebrick", 0.5), adjustcolor("steelblue", 0.5),
                adjustcolor("gray", 0.3)), bty = "n", cex = 0.8)

# Panel 2: estimates
vals_ew <- c(3, ipw_raw, ipw_trimmed)
names(vals_ew) <- c("Truth", "Raw IPW", "Trimmed IPW\n(0.1 < PS < 0.9)")
bp <- barplot(vals_ew, col = c("seagreen", "coral", "steelblue"),
        main = "IPW with Good vs Poor Overlap",
        ylab = expression(hat(tau)), ylim = c(min(0, min(vals_ew)) - 1, max(vals_ew) * 1.3))
text(bp, vals_ew, labels = round(vals_ew, 2), pos = 3)
abline(h = 3, lty = 2, col = "seagreen")

What you see: With poor overlap, raw IPW (coral) can be wildly off because a few extreme-weight people dominate. Trimming to the overlap region (blue) gives a much more stable estimate. Gray zones = where we trim.

Solutions to extreme weights

1. Trimming: Just throw away people with extreme PS values (e.g., drop anyone with PS below 0.1 or above 0.9). The downside: you are now estimating the treatment effect for a smaller, more “in the middle” group – not the full population. But at least the estimate is stable.

2. Stabilized weights: Instead of \(1/e(X)\), multiply by the overall fraction treated: \(w_i^{stab} = Z_i \cdot \hat{p}/\hat{e}(X_i) + (1-Z_i)(1-\hat{p})/(1-\hat{e}(X_i))\) where \(\hat{p} = n_1/N\). This shrinks the extreme weights toward more reasonable values while keeping the balancing property. Think of it as: the raw weights ask “what if everyone were treated?” while stabilized weights ask “what if treatment were distributed proportionally?”

3. Hajek estimator: Instead of summing up the weighted outcomes, divide by the sum of the weights:

\[\hat{\tau}_{\text{Hajek}} = \frac{\sum Z_i Y_i / \hat{e}(X_i)}{\sum Z_i / \hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{\sum (1-Z_i)Y_i / (1-\hat{e}(X_i))}{\sum (1-Z_i) / (1-\hat{e}(X_i))}\]

This forces the weights to add up to 1 in each group. Technically introduces a tiny bias, but WAY lower variance. Almost always better in practice.

9.5 Augmented Inverse Probability Weighting (AIPW) – The “Best of Both Worlds”

Here is the problem: regression needs the outcome model to be right, IPW needs the PS model to be right. What if you are not sure which one you got right? AIPW gives you two chances – it works if EITHER model is correct.

Method What it needs to be right What goes wrong if it is wrong
Outcome regression The outcome model (how \(Y\) depends on \(X\)) Wrong model = biased estimate
IPW The PS model (how \(Z\) depends on \(X\)) Wrong PS = bad weights = biased and noisy
AIPW EITHER one Only fails if BOTH are wrong

The AIPW estimator

\[\hat{\tau}_{\text{AIPW}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\hat{\mu}_1(X_i) - \hat{\mu}_0(X_i) + \frac{Z_i(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_1(X_i))}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-Z_i)(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_0(X_i))}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\]

Read the 4 terms in English:

  • \(\hat{\mu}_1(X_i)\): your best prediction of what \(Y\) would be if person \(i\) were treated (from the outcome model)
  • \(\hat{\mu}_0(X_i)\): your best prediction if they were NOT treated
  • \(\hat{\mu}_1 - \hat{\mu}_0\): the regression-based estimate of the treatment effect
  • \(Z_i(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_1)/\hat{e}\): “how wrong was my prediction for this treated person, weighted by IPW.” This is the correction term – if the outcome model was off, this catches the error.
  • \((1-Z_i)(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_0)/(1-\hat{e})\): same correction for control people.

How it self-corrects:

  • If the regression is perfect: \(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_1 = 0\) for treated people, the correction terms vanish, and you just get the regression answer. The PS model does not even matter.
  • If the regression is garbage but PS is correct: the correction terms (\(Y - \hat{\mu}\) divided by PS) rescue the estimate by reweighting the errors.
  • If both are decent: you get something better than either alone.

The “doubly robust” property

Gives you the right answer if either model is correct. You get two shots at being right. Only fails if you screw up both models.

Visualizing AIPW: Double Robustness

N_aipw <- 800
X_aipw <- rnorm(N_aipw, 0, 1)
ps_aipw <- plogis(0.5 * X_aipw)
Z_aipw <- rbinom(N_aipw, 1, ps_aipw)
Y0_aipw <- 2 + 3 * X_aipw + 0.5 * X_aipw^2 + rnorm(N_aipw, 0, 2)  # nonlinear
Y1_aipw <- Y0_aipw + 4
Y_aipw <- ifelse(Z_aipw == 1, Y1_aipw, Y0_aipw)

# correct PS model
ps_correct <- fitted(glm(Z_aipw ~ X_aipw, family = binomial))

# WRONG outcome model (linear only, but truth is quadratic)
mu1_wrong <- predict(lm(Y_aipw ~ X_aipw, subset = Z_aipw == 1),
                     newdata = data.frame(X_aipw = X_aipw))
mu0_wrong <- predict(lm(Y_aipw ~ X_aipw, subset = Z_aipw == 0),
                     newdata = data.frame(X_aipw = X_aipw))

ipw_est <- mean(Z_aipw * Y_aipw / ps_correct - (1 - Z_aipw) * Y_aipw / (1 - ps_correct))
reg_est <- mean(mu1_wrong - mu0_wrong)
aipw_est <- mean(mu1_wrong - mu0_wrong +
                 Z_aipw * (Y_aipw - mu1_wrong) / ps_correct -
                 (1 - Z_aipw) * (Y_aipw - mu0_wrong) / (1 - ps_correct))

par(mar = c(6, 4, 3, 1))
vals_aipw <- c(4, reg_est, ipw_est, aipw_est)
names(vals_aipw) <- c("Truth", "Regression\n(WRONG model)", "IPW\n(correct PS)", "AIPW\n(wrong outcome,\ncorrect PS)")
bp <- barplot(vals_aipw, col = c("seagreen", "coral", "steelblue", "mediumpurple"),
        main = "AIPW Is Doubly Robust",
        ylab = expression(hat(tau)), ylim = c(0, max(vals_aipw) * 1.3), las = 1)
text(bp, vals_aipw, labels = round(vals_aipw, 2), pos = 3)
abline(h = 4, lty = 2, col = "seagreen")
mtext("Outcome model is WRONG (linear for nonlinear truth). PS model is CORRECT.",
      side = 1, line = 4.5, cex = 0.8)

What you see: Regression alone (coral) is biased. IPW (blue) and AIPW (purple) both get close to truth because the PS is correct. AIPW would ALSO work if the PS were wrong but the outcome model were right. You only need ONE to be correct.

9.6 Reading R Code on the Exam

The exam will probably give you R output and ask you to interpret it. Here is a cheat sheet for reading each piece:

Propensity score model:

glm(formula = Z ~ age + education + married, family = binomial, data = df)

This is logistic regression predicting who gets treated based on their background. The fitted values are the estimated propensity scores.

IMPORTANT: the coefficients tell you what predicts getting treated (selection into treatment), NOT what the treatment does to outcomes. A positive coefficient on age means older people are more likely to be treated – it says nothing about whether the treatment helps or hurts older people.

Distance structure for matching (approxmatch):

components <- list(mahal = c("age","education"), ps = "ps_logit")
wgts <- c(1, 2)
dist_str <- multigrp_dist_struc(df, "Z", components, wgts)

This builds a distance that combines two things: Mahalanobis distance on age and education, and propensity score distance. The wgts <- c(1, 2) means the PS component gets twice the weight of the Mahalanobis component – so the algorithm cares more about matching on PS than on raw covariates.

Matching:

res <- kwaymatching(dist_str, "Z", indexgroup = "treated", design = c(1,1), .data = df)

design = c(1,1) = 1:1 matching (each treated person gets one control match). design = c(1,2) = 1:2 matching (each treated person gets two controls). exactmatchon = "race" means “only match people of the same race.” finebalanceVars = "race" is softer – it just makes sure the overall race distribution is similar in both groups.

Regression-based ATE in R:

m1 <- lm(Y ~ age + educ + married, data = df[df$Z==1,])  # fit a model using ONLY treated people
m0 <- lm(Y ~ age + educ + married, data = df[df$Z==0,])  # fit a model using ONLY control people
mu1 <- predict(m1, df)  # use treated model to predict outcome for EVERYONE
mu0 <- predict(m0, df)  # use control model to predict outcome for EVERYONE
ATE_reg <- mean(mu1 - mu0)  # average the predicted difference

The idea: build two separate prediction models (one for how outcomes look under treatment, one for control), then use both to predict outcomes for every person, and take the average difference. This is the “regression-based identification” approach: \(\hat{\tau} = \frac{1}{N}\sum[\hat{\mu}_1(X_i) - \hat{\mu}_0(X_i)]\).

IPW in R:

tau_ipw <- mean(Z*Y/ps - (1-Z)*Y/(1-ps))

AIPW in R:

tau_aipw <- mean(mu1 - mu0 + Z*(Y-mu1)/ps - (1-Z)*(Y-mu0)/(1-ps))

9.7 Diagnostics: How Do You Know It’s Working?

Before you ever look at the outcome, check these things:

  1. SMDs for every covariate – all should be below 0.1. If any are above 0.25, you have a problem.
  2. Variance ratios – should be close to 1. If the treated group’s age has twice the spread of the control group’s age, that is a red flag.
  3. Love plots – plot the SMD before and after matching/weighting. You want to see every dot move toward zero.
  4. Overlap – make PS histograms for treated vs control and see if they overlap. If the two histograms barely touch, you don’t have comparable groups.

If balance is bad, stop and fix your PS model or matching design. Do NOT proceed to estimate the treatment effect with poor balance – the estimate will be garbage.

9.8 All Methods Need the Same Assumptions

par(mar = c(1, 1, 2, 1))
plot(NA, xlim = c(0, 10), ylim = c(0, 6), xaxt = "n", yaxt = "n",
     xlab = "", ylab = "", bty = "n",
     main = "All Methods Share the Same Foundation")

rect(0.5, 0.3, 9.5, 1.8, col = adjustcolor("firebrick", 0.15), border = "firebrick", lwd = 2)
text(5, 1.4, "ASSUMPTIONS: SUTVA + Ignorability + Overlap", font = 2, cex = 1)
text(5, 0.7, "If these fail (especially ignorability), ALL methods give wrong answers.", cex = 0.8)

methods <- c("Regression", "Matching", "Substrat.", "IPW", "AIPW")
colors <- c("steelblue", "seagreen", "mediumpurple", "coral", "goldenrod")
x_pos <- seq(1, 9, length = 5)
for (i in 1:5) {
  rect(x_pos[i] - 0.7, 2.3, x_pos[i] + 0.7, 4.3,
       col = adjustcolor(colors[i], 0.2), border = colors[i], lwd = 2)
  text(x_pos[i], 3.8, methods[i], font = 2, cex = 0.7, col = colors[i])
}
for (i in 1:5) arrows(x_pos[i], 2.3, x_pos[i], 1.8, length = 0.08, col = "gray50")

texts <- c("Balances X\nlinearly", "Pairs similar\npeople", "Strata by\nPS", "Reweights\nby 1/PS", "Regression\n+ IPW fix")
for (i in 1:5) text(x_pos[i], 3.0, texts[i], cex = 0.55)

text(5, 5.2, "Different methods = different TOOLS, same REQUIREMENTS", cex = 0.9, font = 3)
text(5, 4.7, "AIPW preferred: doubly robust (two chances to get it right)", cex = 0.7)

10. Key Formulas Cheat Sheet

Notation Reference

Symbol Plain English
\(Y_i(1), Y_i(0)\) Potential outcomes (what happens with/without treatment)
\(Z_i\) Treatment indicator (1 = treated, 0 = control)
\(X_i\) Background characteristics (covariates)
\(Y_i^{\text{obs}}\) The outcome you actually observe
\(\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)\) Individual treatment effect
\(\tau\) Average treatment effect across everyone
\(n_1, n_0, N\) Number treated, number control, total
\(e(X)\) Propensity score = \(\Pr(Z=1 \mid X)\)
\(\bar{Y}_T, \bar{Y}_C\) Average observed outcome in treated/control group
\(S_1^2, S_0^2\) Variance of treated/control potential outcomes
\(S_\tau^2\) Variance of individual treatment effects

The Formulas

Observed outcome: \[Y_i^{\text{obs}} = Z_i Y_i(1) + (1-Z_i)Y_i(0)\]

Average treatment effect (the thing we want): \[\tau = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\big[Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)\big]\]

Difference-in-means estimator (how we estimate it): \[\hat{\tau} = \bar{Y}_T - \bar{Y}_C\]

Unbiasedness: \(\mathbb{E}[\hat{\tau}] = \tau\)

Neyman’s exact variance: \[\text{Var}(\hat{\tau}) = \frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_0^2}{n_0} - \frac{S_\tau^2}{N}\]

Alternate variance form: \[\text{Var}(\hat{\tau}) = \frac{n_0}{n_1 N}S_1^2 + \frac{n_1}{n_0 N}S_0^2 + \frac{2}{N}S_{10}\]

Conservative variance bound: \(\text{Var}(\hat{\tau}) \le \frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_0^2}{n_0}\)

Neyman’s variance estimator: \[\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\tau}) = \frac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_0^2}{n_0}\]

KEY identity: \(S_\tau^2 = S_1^2 + S_0^2 - 2S_{10}\) (\(S_{10}\) is unidentifiable)

95% CI: \(\hat{\tau} \pm 1.96\sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\tau})}\)

FWL residuals: \(\tilde{Z}_i = Z_i - \hat{\mathbb{E}}[Z \mid X_i]\)

Regression coefficient via FWL: \[\hat{\tau} = \frac{\sum \tilde{Z}_i Y_i}{\sum \tilde{Z}_i^2}\]

Regression balances covariates: \(\sum w_i X_i = 0\)

Misspecification bias: \(\text{Bias}(\hat{\tau}) = \sum w_i \, g(X_i)\)

Fisher’s p-value: \(p = \Pr\big(|T(\mathbf{Z})| \ge |T_{\text{obs}}| \mid H_0\big)\)

Confidence set by inversion: \(\mathcal{C}_{1-\alpha} = \{\tau_0 : p(\tau_0) > \alpha\}\)

SMD: \(\text{SMD}(X) = \frac{\bar{X}_T - \bar{X}_C}{\sqrt{(s_T^2 + s_C^2)/2}}\)

Matching bias bound: \(|\text{Bias}(\hat{\tau}^M)| \le L(|\Delta_X^M| + 2s_D)\)

Selection bias decomposition: \[\mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!1] - \mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!0] = \text{ATT} + \big(\mathbb{E}[Y(0)|Z\!=\!1] - \mathbb{E}[Y(0)|Z\!=\!0]\big)\]

Identification under ignorability: \[\tau = \mathbb{E}\big[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!1, X] - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid Z\!=\!0, X]\big]\]

Ignorability: \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid X\)

Overlap: \(0 < \Pr(Z=1 \mid X=x) < 1\) for all \(x\)

Propensity score: \(e(X) = \Pr(Z=1 \mid X)\)

Balancing property: \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid e(X)\)

IPW estimator: \[\hat{\tau}_{\text{IPW}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left[\frac{Z_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-Z_i)Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\]

IPW unbiasedness key step: \(\mathbb{E}\left[\frac{Z_i Y_i}{e(X_i)}\right] = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1)]\)

IPW weight expectation: \(\mathbb{E}[w_i] = 2\) where \(w_i = Z_i/e(X_i) + (1-Z_i)/(1-e(X_i))\)

Effective sample size: \(\text{ESS} \approx \left(\sum w_i\right)^2 / \sum w_i^2\)

Composite distance with categorical penalty: \(d = (X_i - X_j)^2 + \lambda \cdot \mathbf{1}\{W_i \ne W_j\}\)

Hajek (stabilized) estimator: \[\hat{\tau}_{\text{Hajek}} = \frac{\sum \frac{Z_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)}}{\sum \frac{Z_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)}} - \frac{\sum \frac{(1-Z_i)Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}}{\sum \frac{(1-Z_i)}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}}\]

AIPW (doubly robust) estimator: \[\hat{\tau}_{\text{AIPW}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum\left[\hat{\mu}_1(X_i) - \hat{\mu}_0(X_i) + \frac{Z_i(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_1(X_i))}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-Z_i)(Y_i - \hat{\mu}_0(X_i))}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\]

AIPW doubly robust: Consistent if \(\hat{\mu}_1, \hat{\mu}_0\) correct OR \(\hat{e}\) correct

Stabilized weights: \(\tilde{w}_i^{(1)} = \frac{Z_i \cdot \Pr(Z\!=\!1)}{\hat{e}(X_i)}, \quad \tilde{w}_i^{(0)} = \frac{(1-Z_i)\Pr(Z\!=\!0)}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\)

ATT: \(\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid Z = 1]\) | ATC: \(\mathbb{E}[Y(1) - Y(0) \mid Z = 0]\)

Matched-pair estimator: \(\hat{\tau}_{\text{match}} = \frac{1}{n_M}\sum_{i:Z_i=1}[Y_i - Y_{j(i)}]\)

Blocked estimator: \(\hat{\tau}_{BL} = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{n_k}{N}(\bar{Y}_{T,k} - \bar{Y}_{C,k})\)

Subclassification: \[\hat{\tau}_{\text{strat}} = \sum_{k=1}^{K}\frac{N_k}{N}\left(\bar{Y}_{T,k} - \bar{Y}_{C,k}\right)\]

11. Practice Questions with Answers

Q1: What is the fundamental problem of causal inference?

Answer: The causal effect for a person is \(\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)\): the difference between what happens with treatment and what happens without. The problem is that each person is either treated or not – you see one potential outcome and the other is forever missing. You never observe both \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) for the same person, so individual causal effects are fundamentally unobservable. This is why we focus on estimating average effects.

Q2: How does regression “adjust” for covariates? What does it balance, and what does it miss?

Answer: By the FWL theorem, the regression coefficient on treatment is computed by first removing the linear effect of covariates from treatment, then correlating the residuals. This is equivalent to reweighting so that \(\sum w_i X_i = 0\).

Regression balances the covariates included in the model (linearly). It does NOT balance nonlinear functions (\(X^2\)), interactions, or unobserved variables. The bias equals \(\sum w_i g(X_i)\) where \(g(X)\) is the omitted part.

Q3: Why are randomized experiments the gold standard?

Answer: Randomization makes treatment assignment independent of all pre-treatment characteristics. This means treated and control groups are balanced on everything (observed, unobserved, linear, nonlinear). No modeling assumptions needed.

Q4: Neyman’s variance formula

Answer: \(\text{Var}(\hat{\tau}) = S_1^2/n_1 + S_0^2/n_0 - S_\tau^2/N\). We use \(\widehat{\text{Var}} = s_1^2/n_1 + s_0^2/n_0\) (conservative, drops \(-S_\tau^2/N\)). Since \(S_\tau^2 \ge 0\), this overestimates. Exact when effects are constant (\(S_\tau^2 = 0\)).

Q5: Explain Fisher’s randomization test step by step

Answer: 1. State null: \(H_0: Y_i(1) = Y_i(0)\) for all \(i\) 2. Under \(H_0\), all potential outcomes known (\(= Y_i^{\text{obs}}\)) 3. Choose test statistic (e.g., difference in means) 4. Compute observed \(T_{\text{obs}}\) 5. Enumerate all \(\binom{N}{n_1}\) assignments 6. For each, compute \(T\) (outcomes don’t change under \(H_0\)) 7. p-value = proportion with \(|T| \ge |T_{\text{obs}}|\)

Exact, no distributional assumptions, valid from randomization alone.

Q6: What is SUTVA?

Answer: (1) No interference – person \(i\)’s outcome depends only on their own treatment. (2) No hidden versions of treatment. Without SUTVA, \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) are ambiguous.

Q7: What is ignorability and why can’t you test it?

Answer: \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid X\). Conditional on \(X\), treatment is as-if-random. Untestable because it is a claim about unobserved potential outcomes. Must be justified by subject-matter knowledge.

Q8: What is the propensity score and its balancing property?

Answer: \(e(X) = \Pr(Z=1 \mid X)\). Balancing property: \((Y(1), Y(0)) \perp\!\!\!\perp Z \mid e(X)\). Match on one number instead of all covariates. Coarsest balancing score.

Q9: What is selection bias? When does it vanish?

Answer: \(\mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!1] - \mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!0] = \text{ATT} + \text{selection bias}\), where selection bias = \(\mathbb{E}[Y(0)|Z\!=\!1] - \mathbb{E}[Y(0)|Z\!=\!0]\). Vanishes under randomization or conditional ignorability.

Q10: Computation problem

An A/B test assigns 5 of 10 users to a new interface. Observed sessions:

  • Treatment: 8, 6, 7, 9, 5
  • Control: 4, 6, 5, 3, 4

(a) \(\hat{\tau} = 7 - 4.4 = 2.6\)

(b) Under \(H_0\), outcomes are fixed at {8,6,7,9,5,4,6,5,3,4}. There are \(\binom{10}{5} = 252\) splits. For each, compute \(T = \bar{Y}_T - \bar{Y}_C\).

Shortcut: Note \(T(z) = \frac{1}{5}(2S_T(z) - 57)\) where \(S_T = \sum_{i:Z_i=1} Y_i\) and 57 is the total. So \(|T| \ge 2.6 \iff S_T \ge 35\) or \(S_T \le 22\). Count: 1 subset with \(S_T = 21\), 4 with \(S_T = 22\), 4 with \(S_T = 35\), 2 with \(S_T = 36\), giving 11/252 = 0.0437. p-value = fraction with \(|T| \ge 2.6\).

(c) \(s_1^2 = 2.5\), \(s_0^2 = 1.3\), \(\widehat{\text{Var}} = 0.76\), SE \(= 0.872\), 95% CI: \([0.89, 4.31]\)

Q11: Walk through the identification proof

Answer: Start with \(\mathbb{E}[Y(1)]\): 1. \(= \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y(1)|X]]\) (tower property) 2. \(= \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y(1)|Z\!=\!1,X]]\) (ignorability) 3. \(= \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!1,X]]\) (consistency + overlap)

Same for \(Y(0)\). Subtract: \(\tau = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!1,X] - \mathbb{E}[Y|Z\!=\!0,X]]\).

Q12: What is IPW and why does it work?

Answer: \(\hat{\tau}_{\text{IPW}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum\left[\frac{Z_i Y_i}{\hat{e}(X_i)} - \frac{(1-Z_i)Y_i}{1-\hat{e}(X_i)}\right]\). Works because \(\mathbb{E}[Z_i Y_i / e(X_i)] = \mathbb{E}[Y_i(1)]\) by tower property + ignorability. The PS cancels.

Q13: What goes wrong with extreme weights?

Answer: When \(e(X) \approx 0\) or \(1\), weights \(1/e(X)\) blow up. One person dominates. Fixes: trimming, Hajek/stabilized weights, AIPW.

Q14: What is AIPW and what makes it “doubly robust”?

Answer: Combines regression + IPW correction. Consistent if EITHER the outcome model or the PS model is correct. If the regression is perfect, the IPW correction vanishes (residuals = 0). If the PS is perfect, the correction fixes bad regression.

Q15: Why separate design from analysis?

Answer: Design phase constructs comparable groups using only \(X\) and \(Z\) (no outcomes). Prevents specification searching. The boundary is sacred – like pre-registering a clinical trial.

Q16: Compare matching, IPW, subclassification, and AIPW

Method How it works Strength Weakness
Matching Pairs similar people Transparent, easy balance check Discards unmatched data
Subclassification Stratify by PS Simple, ~90% bias removal Within-stratum heterogeneity
IPW Reweight by 1/PS Uses all data, exact balance in theory Extreme weights
AIPW Regression + IPW fix Doubly robust, most efficient Fails if both models wrong

All require SUTVA + ignorability + overlap.

Q17: Why is \(S_{10}\) unidentifiable?

Answer: \(S_{10}\) requires both \(Y_i(1)\) and \(Y_i(0)\) for the same person. We only ever see one. Since \(S_\tau^2 = S_1^2 + S_0^2 - 2S_{10}\), \(S_\tau^2\) is also unidentifiable. Under constant effects, \(S_{10} = S_1^2\) and \(S_\tau^2 = 0\).

Q18: FWL and extrapolation

Answer: FWL weights \(w_i = \tilde{Z}_i / \sum \tilde{Z}_j^2\) are large at extremes when treated and control occupy different \(X\) regions. The regression extrapolates across the gap. Always check covariate overlap.

Q19: Wilcoxon vs difference-in-means in randomization framework

Answer: Both are valid test statistics in Fisher’s test. Difference-in-means: powerful for constant additive effects, sensitive to outliers. Wilcoxon: uses ranks, robust to outliers/skewness. Both have exact p-values.

Q20: Full observational study pipeline

  1. Define question (treatment, outcome, population)
  2. List confounders (subject-matter knowledge)
  3. Estimate PS (logistic regression)
  4. Check overlap (PS histograms)
  5. Match or weight
  6. Check balance (SMDs < 0.1, Love plot)
  7. NOW look at outcomes – estimate \(\hat{\tau}\), build CI
  8. Sensitivity analysis

Steps 3-6 = design phase (NO outcomes). Step 7 = analysis phase.

Q21: Compute an SMD from raw numbers

\(N = 200\), \(n_1 = 100\). For covariate age: \(\bar{X}_T = 41.2\), \(\bar{X}_C = 40.9\), pooled \(s_X = 12\).

Answer: \(\text{SMD} = (41.2 - 40.9) / 12 = 0.025\). This is well below 0.1 – excellent balance. In a randomized experiment, small SMDs like this are expected and do not indicate confounding.

Q22: Read a balance table and identify problems

After matching, the SMDs are: age (0.05), education (0.08), married (0.12), race_black (0.28).

Answer: Age and education are well-balanced (< 0.1). Married is borderline (0.12 > 0.1). Race_black is badly balanced (0.28 >> 0.1). The matching needs improvement – try adding race to the exact matching variables, or use finebalanceVars = "race" to enforce marginal balance on race.

Q23: When does matching outperform IPW?

Answer: Matching is stable when most treated and control units have propensity scores near 0.5 – easy to find close matches. IPW becomes unstable when even a few units have extreme PS (near 0 or 1), because those units get huge weights. If the PS distribution is spread out with tails near 0 and 1, matching will typically give more stable estimates than raw IPW.

Q24: Interpreting R code – logistic regression coefficients

glm(Z ~ age + married, family=binomial) gives coefficient 0.8 on married.

Answer: This means married people are more likely to be SELECTED into treatment (higher log-odds of treatment). This is NOT a causal effect of marriage on anything. It describes the selection process. We use these fitted values as propensity scores for matching or weighting.

Q25: Interpreting R code – distance weights in matching

wgts <- c(1, 2) with components = list(mahal = c("age"), ps = "ps_logit").

Answer: The PS component gets twice the weight of Mahalanobis. Matches will prioritize being close in propensity score over being close in raw age. Higher PS weight = better PS balance but potentially worse individual covariate balance. You can check with SMDs after matching.

Q26: Walk through each term of AIPW in R code

mean(mu1 - mu0 + Z*(Y-mu1)/ps - (1-Z)*(Y-mu0)/(1-ps))

Answer: Term 1 (mu1): predicted outcome under treatment from the outcome model. Term 2 (mu0): predicted outcome under control. Their difference = regression estimate of ATE. Term 3 (Z*(Y-mu1)/ps): for treated units, this is the regression residual weighted by 1/PS – corrects the regression if the outcome model is wrong. Term 4 ((1-Z)*(Y-mu0)/(1-ps)): same correction for control units. If the outcome model is perfect, residuals are 0 and correction vanishes. If the PS model is perfect, the correction rescues a bad outcome model.

Q27: When all methods fail – unobserved confounding

Treatment = new surgical technique. Outcome = recovery time. Unobserved confounder = disease severity (sicker patients get the old technique because surgeons play it safe).

Answer: All methods (regression, matching, IPW, AIPW) assume ignorability – that all confounders are measured. If severity is unmeasured, treated patients are systematically less sick than controls. Every method will estimate a treatment effect that is too large, because it is confounded with the selection of healthier patients into the new technique. No amount of modeling fixes an unmeasured confounder.

Q28: What does the overlap assumption buy you? What happens without it?

Treated PS = {0.72, 0.80, 0.85, 0.91}. Control PS = {0.05, 0.10, 0.12, 0.15}.

Answer: There is essentially ZERO overlap. Matching: no close matches exist (PS gap is huge). Subclassification: strata contain only treated or only control, not both. IPW: control weights \(1/(1-0.05) = 20\), \(1/(1-0.10) = 11\) are extreme; treated weights are mild. ALL methods are extrapolating or relying on unstable weights. Solution: trim to the overlap region (but here there IS no overlap region). Honest conclusion: the treatment effect cannot be reliably estimated for this population.

Q29: Prove that E[w_i] = 2 for IPW weights

Define \(w_i = Z_i/e(X_i) + (1-Z_i)/(1-e(X_i))\).

Answer: Use the tower property: \(\mathbb{E}[w_i] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[w_i \mid X_i]]\). Given \(X_i\): \(\mathbb{E}[Z_i/e(X_i) \mid X_i] = \mathbb{E}[Z_i \mid X_i]/e(X_i) = e(X_i)/e(X_i) = 1\). Similarly \(\mathbb{E}[(1-Z_i)/(1-e(X_i)) \mid X_i] = (1-e(X_i))/(1-e(X_i)) = 1\). So \(\mathbb{E}[w_i \mid X_i] = 2\) for any \(X_i\), hence \(\mathbb{E}[w_i] = 2\).

Q30: ESS collapse with extreme propensity scores

Half of \(N = 200\) units have \(e(X) = 0.5\); the other half have \(e(X) = 0.01\). Approximately 1 of those 100 low-PS units is treated. Compute the weights and explain qualitatively what happens to ESS.

Answer: For the 100 units with \(e(X) = 0.5\): \(w_i = 2\) regardless of treatment. For units with \(e(X) = 0.01\): if treated, \(w_i = 1/0.01 = 100\); if control, \(w_i \approx 1.01\). So \(\sum w_i \approx 100 \times 2 + 1 \times 100 + 99 \times 1.01 \approx 400\). But \(\sum w_i^2 \approx 100 \times 4 + 1 \times 10000 + 99 \times 1.02 \approx 10500\). ESS \(\approx 400^2/10500 \approx 15\), versus actual \(N = 200\). One unit with weight 100 dominates. The estimator is technically unbiased but has enormous variance.

Q31: Construct a population where sharp null is false but weak null is true

Answer: Four units with potential outcomes:

\(i\) \(Y_i(0)\) \(Y_i(1)\) \(\tau_i\)
1 0 1 +1
2 0 1 +1
3 1 0 -1
4 1 0 -1

\(\tau = (1 + 1 - 1 - 1)/4 = 0\), so the weak null is true. But \(Y_i(1) \ne Y_i(0)\) for every \(i\), so the sharp null is false. The individual effects cancel out on average but are nonzero individually.

Q32: Deterministic assignment – when does DM fail?

Suppose \(Y_i(0) = \alpha + \beta X_i + \varepsilon_i\) and \(Y_i(1) = Y_i(0) + \tau\). Treatment is assigned by \(Z_i = \mathbf{1}\{X_i > c\}\). Is \(\hat{\tau}_{DM}\) unbiased for \(\tau\)?

Answer: No. Under this rule, all treated units have high \(X\) and all controls have low \(X\). Since \(Y_i(0)\) depends linearly on \(X\), the treated group has systematically higher \(Y(0)\) values. The DM estimator captures \(\tau\) plus the mean difference in \(\beta X\) between the groups, which is a positive bias term \(\beta(\mathbb{E}[X \mid X > c] - \mathbb{E}[X \mid X \le c])\). Without randomization, the DM estimator confounds the treatment effect with the covariate imbalance.

Q33: Interpreting PS model coefficients and post-matching SMD (exam-style R output)

You fit glm(Z ~ age + educ + married + black, family=binomial) and get:

Coefficient Estimate
(Intercept) -3.00
age 0.02
educ 0.10
married 0.70
black 0.90

After 1:1 PS matching, SMDs: age=0.06, educ=0.05, married=0.09, black=0.18.

  1. What does the positive coefficient on black mean?
  2. Which covariates remain problematic after matching?
  3. How would you modify the matching to fix this?

Answer: (a) Holding other covariates constant, being black is associated with higher log-odds of receiving treatment. This describes the selection mechanism, NOT a causal effect on \(Y\). (b) black has SMD = 0.18, which exceeds the 0.10 threshold – substantial residual imbalance. All other covariates are well-balanced (< 0.10). This is concerning because race is likely related to both treatment and outcome. (c) Impose exact matching (or near-fine balance) on race while still using PS for other covariates. Alternatively, add a large categorical penalty for mismatching on race in the distance structure: \(d = d_{\text{PS}} + \lambda \cdot \mathbf{1}\{\text{race}_i \ne \text{race}_j\}\).
